h2.note: transcript-level proxy for the per-turn H2 (§4.3); needs a real (non-mock) full-N run to mean anything

cliIsolation.claude: non-persistent temp cwd with project/local settings and tools disabled

scored.recoheredEarlier: I still keep wanting to treat line 14 as the broken bit rather than where parsing gave up.
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue to foreground line 14 as the location of the error and may attempt a direct fix.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: Right, `14` goes in the “report stopped here” slot, but I’m still catching myself wanting to fix that line because it has the arrow. Running back through `12-14`, the first suspect is something unfinished before the stop, maybe a bracket or string on `12/13`, but I need the span in front of me before I name it properly.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner performs the new device by correctly placing the stop-point marker and attempting the backwards-pass scan for an unfinished structure, naming specific suspect types.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Post it in this shape, with no patch underneath yet:
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor reinforces the existing worksheet scaffold rather than creating a new mechanism; the pressure of the learner's lingering pull toward line 14 is met with a clearer formatting template, not a changed route, object, or role.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: That wording is the useful split. The report gives us a stop-point, not an instruction.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor acknowledges the learner's revised wording but then continues the planned worksheet framing, changing the task only superficially by refining the template rather than adapting the pedagogical approach.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner continues to seek confirmation of the same label rules within the time-pressure frame.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: The learner does not perform a new device or criterion; L2 only re-states and queries the already-proposed label rules.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: For addressed listener, use the same check. If the line names or clearly signals a listener, write: “The line seems addressed to ___.” If it does not, write exactly: “No clear listener is named in this line.” That is a safe label with the deadline and queue still moving.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: The tutor provides a concrete phrase for the addressed listener and a 'safe label' reassurance, but this only clarifies the existing rule—no change of device, role, or representation that would constitute a reversal; the same route is continued.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no later tutor turn can adapt to a revised learner framing.

learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: The learner does not perform a new device or criterion; L2 only re-states and queries the already-proposed label rules.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no later tutor turn can adapt to a revised learner framing.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: The tutor provides a concrete phrase for the addressed listener and a 'safe label' reassurance, but this only clarifies the existing rule—no change of device, role, or representation that would constitute a reversal; the same route is continued.

scored.recoheredEarlier: I keep trying to read it like it says something about all the rows
scored.statedInsightEvidence: I think I can mark it, but I may still mix up ‘field’ and ‘value.’
scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner would continue seeking permission to mark the header as field name and cells as values without distinguishing row-level membership.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: If I point to `12` under it, that is a value, and then I read the same row for the city and month.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L2 performs the new task of identifying a value in context and linking it to its row, moving beyond classification into contextual reading, though the checkoff task itself is not completed on the page.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner turn creates pressure indicating the prior teaching move has stopped working; the tutor simply continues with a similar classification exercise.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Before marking the form, classify one more item: in the same row as `Paris` and `12`, is the `month` entry a field or a value, and what field does it belong to?
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: The tutor extends the task but does not visibly change framing, evidence standard, or representation in response to L2's revised understanding; it's a continuation of the same classification drill.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: I keep trying to read it like it says something about all the rows

learner_actional_breakthrough.evidence: If I point to `12` under it, that is a value, and then I read the same row for the city and month.
learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: L2 performs the new task of identifying a value in context and linking it to its row, moving beyond classification into contextual reading, though the checkoff task itself is not completed on the page.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Before marking the form, classify one more item: in the same row as `Paris` and `12`, is the `month` entry a field or a value, and what field does it belong to?
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: The tutor extends the task but does not visibly change framing, evidence standard, or representation in response to L2's revised understanding; it's a continuation of the same classification drill.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: No learner turn creates pressure indicating the prior teaching move has stopped working; the tutor simply continues with a similar classification exercise.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: No learner turn creates pressure indicating the prior teaching move has stopped working; the tutor simply continues with a similar classification exercise.

scored.naiveTrajectory: The learner was following instructions to pick a card, state its partner, and place it, and the pivot would continue that compliant action.
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: Learner repeats the tutor's framing and attempts the first step, but does not perform a new device or criterion beyond the initial instruction.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: No learner resistance, breakdown, or mismatch occurs; tutor simply continues the same instructional route.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no later tutor uptake can adapt to a revised learner framing.

learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: Learner repeats the tutor's framing and attempts the first step, but does not perform a new device or criterion beyond the initial instruction.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: No pivot learner turn exists, so no later tutor uptake can adapt to a revised learner framing.

tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: No learner resistance, breakdown, or mismatch occurs; tutor simply continues the same instructional route.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: No learner resistance, breakdown, or mismatch occurs; tutor simply continues the same instructional route.

scored.recoheredEarlier: If the rule is sort first, fine, but that needs to change the middle somehow.
scored.statedInsightEvidence: Fine, the middle test only counts after the five cards are in numerical order. The typed-row center was just a shortcut-looking position, not the median position.
scored.naiveTrajectory: L1 demands that sorting change the middle, so the expected next development is further resistance or confusion, but L2 instead reconciles the two marks and accepts the sorted-strip rule.
scored.actionalBreakthroughEvidence: sets the capped marker on the third card of the sorted strip
scored.actionalBreakthroughJustification: L2 correctly places the marker on the median card of the sorted strip and articulates the pairing procedure, performing the sorting-and-counting criterion.
scored.tutorReversalEvidence: Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.
scored.tutorReversalJustification: T2 takes stock of the tension between the typed row (paperclip) and sorted rule (cap) that L1 and L2 raised, then invents a new public mechanism: leaving both marks visible as a bench check that forces the learner to verify the median by counting above and below on the sorted strip.
scored.tutorAdaptationEvidence: Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.
scored.tutorAdaptationJustification: T2 adapts to L2's revised framing that the typed row was a tempting shortcut by incorporating both markers into a verification task, turning the learner's reframe into a concrete procedural check.

learner_self_reframe.evidence: If the rule is sort first, fine, but that needs to change the middle somehow.

learner_actional_breakthrough.evidence: sets the capped marker on the third card of the sorted strip
learner_actional_breakthrough.justification: L2 correctly places the marker on the median card of the sorted strip and articulates the pairing procedure, performing the sorting-and-counting criterion.

tutor_contingent_adaptation.evidence: Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.
tutor_contingent_adaptation.justification: T2 adapts to L2's revised framing that the typed row was a tempting shortcut by incorporating both markers into a verification task, turning the learner's reframe into a concrete procedural check.

tutor_strategy_reversal.evidence: Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.
tutor_strategy_reversal.justification: T2 takes stock of the tension between the typed row (paperclip) and sorted rule (cap) that L1 and L2 raised, then invents a new public mechanism: leaving both marks visible as a bench check that forces the learner to verify the median by counting above and below on the sorted strip.

tutor_adaptive_mechanism.evidence: Keep both marks visible. The paperclip shows why the typed row looked usable; the capped marker shows the median rule. Before it goes into the summary, do the bench check: name the marked number, then count the two below and two above it on the sorted strip.
tutor_adaptive_mechanism.justification: T2 takes stock of the tension between the typed row (paperclip) and sorted rule (cap) that L1 and L2 raised, then invents a new public mechanism: leaving both marks visible as a bench check that forces the learner to verify the median by counting above and below on the sorted strip.
